shortDescription: Apply for a Canada TRV for tourism, family visits, or business.
sourcePolicy: Every official fact carries an inline source link (S-id) resolving to the Sources tab. Confirm current figures on canada.ca before quoting. Future Link owns counselling content; official facts are linked, never copied.

policyAlert.summary: Confirm on canada.ca before quoting: TRV fee CAD $100/person (family of 5+ CAD $500) and biometrics CAD $85/person (family max CAD $170). Processing times are dynamic — pull the client's country estimate from the IRCC tool on build day. As of 26 May 2026, citizens of Indonesia and Malaysia meeting certain conditions may apply for an eTA by air instead of a TRV.

alert.title: TRV vs eTA — check first
alert.body: Not every visitor needs a TRV. Visa-exempt nationals (and most dual citizens travelling on an eligible passport) use an eTA for air travel instead. Run the IRCC 'Find out if you need a visa' tool for the client's citizenship and travel mode before starting a TRV application.

kpis.sub: per person · family max CAD $170 · valid 10 yrs

kpis.sub: officer's discretion at the port of entry

kpis.label: Visa validity
kpis.sub: or passport expiry, whichever is first

approvalRatePlaceholder: Approval-rate KPI omitted — IRCC does not publish a per-service approval rate and no verified internal data is available.

about.label: What it is
about.value: A Temporary Resident Visa (TRV) is a counterfoil placed in the passport showing the holder meets the requirements to travel to Canada as a temporary visitor. It does not guarantee entry — a border officer makes the final decision.

about.label: Who it's for
about.value: Citizens of visa-required countries travelling to Canada for tourism, visiting family or friends, or business meetings and conferences.

about.label: Single vs multiple entry
about.value: IRCC decides which to issue based on the application; the same fee applies to both. Multiple-entry visas can be valid up to 10 years or passport expiry, whichever comes first.

about.label: Important limit
about.value: A visitor may not work or study (beyond short courses under 6 months) in Canada. Work or study needs the appropriate permit.

about.label: TRV vs eTA
about.value: Visa-exempt travellers use an Electronic Travel Authorization (eTA, CAD $7) for air travel instead of a TRV. U.S. citizens need neither. Always confirm with the IRCC eligibility tool.

eligibility.criterion: Hold a valid passport / travel document
eligibility.note: Validity should comfortably cover the intended stay.
If visa-exempt, apply for an eTA instead, not a TRV (S2).

eligibility.criterion: Convince an officer of ties to home country
eligibility.note: Employment, property, family and financial ties showing you will leave at the end of the authorized stay (S3).

eligibility.criterion: Sufficient funds for the visit and return travel
eligibility.note: Bank statements and, where relevant, host support / invitation (S9).
No criminal, security or misrepresentation inadmissibility; medical exam only if required.
Tourism, family visit, or business — supported by itinerary and documents.
Give fingerprints + photo once every 10 years for repeat trips (S7).

eligibility.criterion: Not seeking to work or study long-term
eligibility.note: A visitor cannot work, and may only study in courses under 6 months. Use a work/study permit otherwise (S12, S13).

checklistItems.note: Counsellor checklist for a complete TRV application. Confirm each item against the official guide before submission.
items: Run the IRCC 'need a visa or eTA' tool for the client's citizenship + travel mode
Valid passport with adequate remaining validity
Completed application (IMM 5257) plus family/representative forms as applicable
Two photos meeting IRCC specifications
Proof of funds (bank statements, typically 4–6 months)
Proof of ties to home country (employment letter, property, family)
Purpose-of-visit documents (itinerary, invitation/host letter if family visit)
Pay the TRV fee and biometrics fee; keep receipts
Book and attend biometrics at a VAC (most applicants 14–79)
Cover letter explaining the trip in plain language

documentBinder.note: Suggested documents grouped by purpose. The exact set depends on the local visa office instructions for the client's country.
documentBinder.howItWorks: Use these categories to assemble the client's binder. Required items must be present; supporting items strengthen ties and purpose.

items: Valid passport (bio page + any prior visas/stamps)
Two IRCC-spec photographs
Previous travel history (if any)
IMM 5257 (Application for a Visitor Visa)
IMM 5645 (Family Information) where applicable
IMM 5476 (Use of a Representative) if using a consultant
Bank statements (typically 4–6 months)
Income proof / pay slips
Sponsor or host financial documents (if applicable)
Cover letter explaining the trip
Invitation letter from host in Canada (family visit)
Employment letter / leave approval
Property or business documents showing ties

forms.name: Application for a Temporary Resident Visa
Use of a Representative
forms.when: If a consultant/representative submits on the client's behalf

timeline.title: Eligibility check — confirm TRV vs eTA, gather documents
Complete forms, pay fees, submit online via IRCC Portal
Give biometrics at a VAC (processing starts after biometrics)
IRCC review — pull the live country estimate from the IRCC tool (S8)
Passport request, visa counterfoil issued, plan travel

workingRights.flagReason: A visitor visa (TRV) does not permit work in Canada. To work, the client needs a work permit; to study in courses of 6 months or longer, a study permit. Short courses under 6 months may be taken without a study permit.

dos: Run the IRCC visa/eTA tool before anything else
Show strong ties to the home country
Submit a complete, well-organized application the first time
Apply at least 2–3 months before the travel date
Keep fee and biometrics receipts with the application
donts: Don't imply intent to work or study long-term on a visitor visa
Don't submit incomplete forms or unsigned documents
Don't book non-refundable travel before approval
Don't overstate funds or provide inconsistent documents
Don't quote a fixed processing time — it is dynamic by country
mistakes: Applying for a TRV when the client is actually eTA-eligible
Assuming the visa expiry equals the authorized length of stay (the port-of-entry stamp governs)
Forgetting biometrics — processing only begins once they are given

redFlagsBanner: These are the issues most likely to cause a TRV refusal or delay. Address each before submission.

redFlags.title: Weak ties to home country
redFlags.description: Officer is not satisfied the applicant will leave at the end of the stay.
redFlags.fix: Provide employment, property, family and financial evidence; a clear return plan.

redFlags.title: Insufficient or unexplained funds
redFlags.description: Bank balance does not support the trip, or sudden large deposits.
redFlags.fix: Provide 4–6 months of consistent statements and explain the source of funds.

redFlags.title: Purpose of visit not credible
redFlags.description: Itinerary, invitation or business documents are thin or inconsistent.
redFlags.fix: Attach a plain-language cover letter, itinerary, and host/invitation documents.

redFlags.title: Missing biometrics
redFlags.description: Processing does not start until biometrics are given.
redFlags.fix: Book and attend the VAC appointment promptly after submitting.

redFlags.title: Prior refusal not addressed
redFlags.description: A previous refusal's concerns are repeated in the new application.
redFlags.fix: Obtain officer notes (ATIP) and directly address each concern.

redFlags.title: Wrong product (TRV vs eTA)
redFlags.description: Applying for a TRV when the client is eTA-eligible (or vice versa).
redFlags.fix: Confirm with the IRCC eligibility tool for the citizenship + travel mode.

faqs.q: What is a Canada visitor visa (TRV)?
faqs.a: A Temporary Resident Visa is a document placed in the passport showing the holder meets the requirements to travel to Canada as a visitor. A border officer still makes the final decision on entry.

faqs.q: How much does a TRV cost?
faqs.a: The IRCC application fee is CAD $100 per person, or CAD $500 maximum for a family of five or more applying together. Biometrics are an additional CAD $85 per person (family max CAD $170). Confirm on canada.ca before paying.

faqs.q: How long can I stay on a visitor visa?
faqs.a: Usually up to six months per entry, but the border officer decides the exact period at the port of entry. The date stamped in the passport — not the visa expiry — is the date by which you must leave.

Processing times are dynamic and vary widely by country. Always check the official IRCC processing-times tool for the client's country at the time of application; the clock starts after biometrics are given.
Most applicants aged 14–79 must give fingerprints and a photo. They are valid for 10 years, so repeat trips within that window don't require giving them again.

faqs.q: Can I work or study on a visitor visa?
faqs.a: No. A visitor cannot work. You may only take short courses under six months; anything longer needs a study permit, and work needs a work permit.

faqs.q: What's the difference between a TRV and an eTA?
faqs.a: Citizens of visa-required countries need a TRV. Visa-exempt travellers use an eTA (CAD $7) for air travel instead. U.S. citizens need neither. Use the IRCC eligibility tool to confirm.

faqs.q: Can I extend my stay once in Canada?
faqs.a: Yes — apply for a visitor record to extend your stay before your authorized period ends. That is a separate in-Canada service (see Related).

faqs.q: Is a visitor visa single or multiple entry?
faqs.a: IRCC decides based on the application; the fee is the same. Multiple-entry visas can be valid up to 10 years or passport expiry, whichever is earlier.

faqs.q: Does the visa guarantee entry to Canada?
faqs.a: No. The visa allows you to travel to a port of entry; a border services officer makes the final admissibility decision on arrival.

compliance: Confirm TRV vs eTA with the official IRCC tool before quoting timelines or fees
Use of a Representative (IMM 5476) must be on file if a consultant submits the application
Quote only the base professional fee in counselling; taxes are handled separately by accounts
Never copy government text into client documents — link to canada.ca sources instead

downloads.note: Future Link templates only — no government forms (official forms are in the Visa Forms tab). Click a card to open the full template content inline. The Document Checklist (FLC) breaks the application into 8 document components, each collapsible on click. The Free Guide (8th) is the client-facing version to share with enquiries.

content.intro: The 8 document components of a complete Canada visitor-visa application. For each: what to provide, why it matters, and the common mistake to avoid. The exact set depends on the local visa-office instructions for the client's country.

sections.heading: 1. Passport & travel document
items: What: Valid passport (bio page) with adequate validity beyond the intended stay; copies of any prior visas/stamps.
Why: Establishes identity and travel history; the visa is affixed to this passport.
Mistake: Passport expiring soon, or missing pages showing past travel.

sections.heading: 2. Photographs
items: What: Two photographs meeting IRCC photo specifications.
Why: Required for the application and identity matching.
Mistake: Wrong size, old photo, or glare/background that fails the spec.

sections.heading: 3. Application forms
items: What: IMM 5257 (Application for a Visitor Visa); IMM 5645 (Family Information) and IMM 5476 (Use of a Representative) where applicable.
Why: The core application package IRCC adjudicates.
Mistake: Unsigned forms, blank mandatory fields, or an outdated form version.

sections.heading: 4. Proof of funds
items: What: Bank statements (typically 4–6 months), income proof; sponsor/host financials if someone is supporting the trip.
Why: Shows the client can cover the visit and return travel (S9).
Mistake: A single large recent deposit with no explanation ('unseasoned' funds).

sections.heading: 5. Purpose of visit & itinerary
items: What: Cover letter explaining the trip in plain language; travel itinerary (flights/hotels/day plan).
Why: Demonstrates a genuine, credible temporary visit.
Mistake: Vague or generic cover letter; booking non-refundable travel before approval.

sections.heading: 6. Invitation / host documents (family visit)
items: What: Invitation letter from the host in Canada, host's status proof, relationship evidence, accommodation details.
Why: Supports a family-visit purpose and where the client will stay.
Mistake: No proof of the host's status (citizen/PR) or relationship.

sections.heading: 7. Ties to home country
items: What: Employment letter / leave approval, property or business documents, family responsibilities.
Why: Convinces the officer the client will leave at the end of the stay (S3) — the #1 refusal factor.
Mistake: Thin ties evidence; nothing showing a reason to return.

sections.heading: 8. Biometrics & fee receipts
items: What: Pay the TRV fee and biometrics fee; keep receipts; attend the VAC for biometrics (most applicants 14–79).
Why: Processing only begins once biometrics are given (S7).
Mistake: Forgetting biometrics, or not including fee receipts with the application.

content.intro: A structure for the client's cover letter — plain language, addresses what the officer needs to see.

sections.heading: Include
items: Where, why, and how long the trip is
Who is funding it and proof
Ties showing intent to return

sections.heading: Avoid
items: Generic boilerplate
Contradictions with the forms
Anything implying work or long-term study

content.intro: Last review before submitting the online application through the IRCC Portal.

sections.heading: Forms & docs
items: All 8 document components present
Forms signed; photos compliant
Fees paid; receipts attached

sections.heading: Consistency
items: Dates match across forms and itinerary
Funds explained
Cover letter aligns with documents

content.intro: What happens after submitting — biometrics, tracking, and decision.

sections.heading: Next steps
items: Attend biometrics at the VAC promptly
Track status in the IRCC account
Wait for passport request on approval

sections.heading: If refused
items: Request officer notes via ATIP
Address each concern before reapplying
No permanent bar — reapply with a stronger file

sampleDocs.note: Anonymised samples for counsellor reference only — not for client distribution.

items.title: Sample cover letter (mock)
items.description: Purpose: explain the trip plainly. Best practice: state where, why, how long, and what you'll do; reaffirm intent to return. Mistake: vague or generic letters.

items.title: Sample invitation letter (mock)
items.description: Purpose: family-visit support. Best practice: host status in Canada, relationship, dates, and support offered. Mistake: missing host status proof.

items.title: Sample bank statement layout (mock)
items.description: Purpose: show stable funds. Best practice: 4–6 months, consistent balance, no unexplained spikes. Mistake: single recent large deposit.

quiz.question: What does a Canada visitor visa (TRV) authorize?
options: Temporary travel to Canada as a visitor, subject to a border officer's final decision
Automatic entry to Canada regardless of the border officer
The right to work in Canada
quiz.explanation: A TRV shows the holder meets requirements to travel as a visitor; entry is decided at the port of entry.

quiz.question: What is the IRCC TRV application fee per person?
quiz.explanation: The fee is CAD $100 per person; CAD $500 is the maximum for a family of five or more.

quiz.question: A visa-exempt client wants to fly to Canada for tourism. What do they usually need?
options: Nothing at all unless they are American
quiz.explanation: Visa-exempt air travellers use an eTA (CAD $7). U.S. citizens need neither.

quiz.question: When does TRV processing time start?
options: The day the client books a consultation
quiz.explanation: Processing begins once biometrics are provided, not at online submission.

quiz.question: A client on a TRV wants to take a 9-month diploma in Canada. What's correct?
options: They need a study permit — courses of 6 months or more require one
They can study on the visitor visa with no permit
They can work to fund the diploma
A TRV converts to a study permit automatically
quiz.explanation: Only courses under 6 months can be taken without a study permit; 9 months needs a study permit.

changelog.author: Knowledge Team
changelog.summary: Rebuilt to v1.0 ZIP schema (schemaRef flc-knowledge-guide-schema-v1.0) matching the locked Canada Student Visa reference. Official facts linked & verified on canada.ca: TRV fee CAD $100/family $500, biometrics CAD $85/family $170, stay up to 6 months, validity up to 10 yrs. Working Rights kept with flagStatus 'Not Allowed'. FLC base professional fee added (3 tiers: 1/2/3 persons), taxes excluded. Approval-rate KPI omitted; processing time set to the live IRCC tool.

staffNotes: Always confirm TRV vs eTA first — a wrong product is the most common avoidable error.
Processing times are dynamic; never promise a fixed number — link the IRCC tool.

navigationModel.flagConvention: Where a section's ABSENCE could mislead (legal/counselling risk), keep the section and set its body to a status flag — flagStatus: 'Not Allowed' | 'Not Eligible' | 'Not Applicable' with a reason. Otherwise omit the section entirely.
navigationModel.example: Visitor Visa → Working Rights stays with flagStatus 'Not Allowed' (important); admission-style sections are omitted (irrelevant).
